question: A-3. Consider polynomial forms \( a x^{2}-b x+c \) with integer coefficients which have two distinct zeros in the open interval \( 0<x<1 \). Exhibit with a proof the least positive integer value of \( a \) for which such a polynomial exists.
solution:
A-3 Let \( f(x)=a x^{2}-b x+c=a(x-r)(x-s) \). Then \( f(0) \cdot f(1)=a^{2} r(r-1) \) \( \cdot s(s-1) \). The graph of \( r(r-1) \) shows that \( 0<r<1 \) implies \( 0<r(r-1) \leqq \frac{1}{4} \), with equality if and only if \( r=\frac{1}{2} \). Similarly, \( 0<s(s-1) \leqq \frac{1}{4} \). Since \( r \neq s, r(r-1) s(s-1) \) \( <1 / 16 \) and \( 0<f(0) \cdot f(1)<a^{2} / 16 \). The coefficients \( a, b, c \) are integers and thus \( 1 \leqq f(0) \cdot f(1) \). Consequently \( a^{2}>16 \), i.e. \( a \geqq 5 \).

The discriminant \( b^{2}-4 a c \) shows that the minimum possible value for \( b \) is 5 . Furthermore, \( 5 x^{2}-5 x+1 \) has two distinct roots between 0 and 1.

descriptive_long.question: A-3. Consider polynomial forms \( leading variable^{2}-middle variable+constant \) with integer coefficients which have two distinct zeros in the open interval \( 0<variable<1 \). Exhibit with a proof the least positive integer value of \( leading \) for which such a polynomial exists.
descriptive_long.solution:
A-3 Let \( function(variable)=leading variable^{2}-middle variable+constant=leading(variable-firstroot)(variable-secondroot) \). Then \( function(0) \cdot function(1)=leading^{2} firstroot(firstroot-1) \) \( \cdot secondroot(secondroot-1) \). The graph of \( firstroot(firstroot-1) \) shows that \( 0<firstroot<1 \) implies \( 0<firstroot(firstroot-1) \leqq \frac{1}{4} \), with equality if and only if \( firstroot=\frac{1}{2} \). Similarly, \( 0<secondroot(secondroot-1) \leqq \frac{1}{4} \). Since \( firstroot \neq secondroot, firstroot(firstroot-1) secondroot(secondroot-1) \) \( <1 / 16 \) and \( 0<function(0) \cdot function(1)<leading^{2} / 16 \). The coefficients \( leading, middle, constant \) are integers and thus \( 1 \leqq function(0) \cdot function(1) \). Consequently \( leading^{2}>16 \), i.e. \( leading \geqq 5 \).

The discriminant \( middle^{2}-4 leading constant \) shows that the minimum possible value for \( middle \) is 5. Furthermore, \( 5 variable^{2}-5 variable+1 \) has two distinct roots between 0 and 1.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: A-3. Consider polynomial forms \( lighthouse elderberry^{2}-tambourine elderberry+bookshelf \) with integer coefficients which have two distinct zeros in the open interval \( 0<elderberry<1 \). Exhibit with a proof the least positive integer value of \( lighthouse \) for which such a polynomial exists.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-3 Let \( firestone(elderberry)=lighthouse elderberry^{2}-tambourine elderberry+bookshelf=lighthouse(elderberry-sandstone)(elderberry-snowflake) \). Then \( firestone(0) \cdot firestone(1)=lighthouse^{2} sandstone(sandstone-1) \) \( \cdot snowflake(snowflake-1) \). The graph of \( sandstone(sandstone-1) \) shows that \( 0<sandstone<1 \) implies \( 0<sandstone(sandstone-1) \leqq \frac{1}{4} \), with equality if and only if \( sandstone=\frac{1}{2} \). Similarly, \( 0<snowflake(snowflake-1) \leqq \frac{1}{4} \). Since \( sandstone \neq snowflake, sandstone(sandstone-1) snowflake(snowflake-1) \) \( <1 / 16 \) and \( 0<firestone(0) \cdot firestone(1)<lighthouse^{2} / 16 \). The coefficients \( lighthouse, tambourine, bookshelf \) are integers and thus \( 1 \leqq firestone(0) \cdot firestone(1) \). Consequently \( lighthouse^{2}>16 \), i.e. \( lighthouse \geqq 5 \).

The discriminant \( tambourine^{2}-4 lighthouse bookshelf \) shows that the minimum possible value for \( tambourine \) is 5 . Furthermore, \( 5 elderberry^{2}-5 elderberry+1 \) has two distinct roots between 0 and 1.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: A-3. Consider polynomial forms \( endcoeff constantval^{2}-edgecoeff constantval+varyingterm \) with integer coefficients which have two distinct zeros in the open interval \( 0<constantval<1 \). Exhibit with a proof the least positive integer value of \( endcoeff \) for which such a polynomial exists.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-3 Let \( staticval(constantval)=endcoeff constantval^{2}-edgecoeff constantval+varyingterm=endcoeff(constantval-crownpoint)(constantval-leafnode) \). Then \( staticval(0) \cdot staticval(1)=endcoeff^{2} crownpoint(crownpoint-1) \cdot leafnode(leafnode-1) \). The graph of \( crownpoint(crownpoint-1) \) shows that \( 0<crownpoint<1 \) implies \( 0<crownpoint(crownpoint-1) \leqq \frac{1}{4} \), with equality if and only if \( crownpoint=\frac{1}{2} \). Similarly, \( 0<leafnode(leafnode-1) \leqq \frac{1}{4} \). Since \( crownpoint \neq leafnode, crownpoint(crownpoint-1) leafnode(leafnode-1) <1 / 16 \) and \( 0<staticval(0) \cdot staticval(1)<endcoeff^{2} / 16 \). The coefficients \( endcoeff, edgecoeff, varyingterm \) are integers and thus \( 1 \leqq staticval(0) \cdot staticval(1) \). Consequently \( endcoeff^{2}>16 \), i.e. \( endcoeff \geqq 5 \).

The discriminant \( edgecoeff^{2}-4 endcoeff varyingterm \) shows that the minimum possible value for \( edgecoeff \) is 5 . Furthermore, \( 5 constantval^{2}-5 constantval+1 \) has two distinct roots between 0 and 1.

garbled_string.question: Consider polynomial forms \( xmnfgqrz qzxwvtnp^{2}-ldkjshvw qzxwvtnp+vhrgploe \) with integer coefficients which have two distinct zeros in the open interval \( 0<qzxwvtnp<1 \). Exhibit with a proof the least positive integer value of \( xmnfgqrz \) for which such a polynomial exists.
garbled_string.solution:
A-3 Let \( bznptkwe(qzxwvtnp)=xmnfgqrz qzxwvtnp^{2}-ldkjshvw qzxwvtnp+vhrgploe=xmnfgqrz(qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla)(qzxwvtnp-mvcldrqo) \). Then \( bznptkwe(0) \cdot bznptkwe(1)=xmnfgqrz^{2} hjgrksla(hjgrksla-1) \cdot mvcldrqo(mvcldrqo-1) \). The graph of \( hjgrksla(hjgrksla-1) \) shows that \( 0<hjgrksla<1 \) implies \( 0<hjgrksla(hjgrksla-1) \leqq \frac{1}{4} \), with equality if and only if \( hjgrksla=\frac{1}{2} \). Similarly, \( 0<mvcldrqo(mvcldrqo-1) \leqq \frac{1}{4} \). Since \( hjgrksla \neq mvcldrqo, hjgrksla(hjgrksla-1) mvcldrqo(mvcldrqo-1) <1 / 16 \) and \( 0<bznptkwe(0) \cdot bznptkwe(1)<xmnfgqrz^{2} / 16 \). The coefficients \( xmnfgqrz, ldkjshvw, vhrgploe \) are integers and thus \( 1 \leqq bznptkwe(0) \cdot bznptkwe(1) \). Consequently \( xmnfgqrz^{2}>16 \), i.e. \( xmnfgqrz \geqq 5 \).

The discriminant \( ldkjshvw^{2}-4 xmnfgqrz vhrgploe \) shows that the minimum possible value for \( ldkjshvw \) is 5. Furthermore, \( 5 qzxwvtnp^{2}-5 qzxwvtnp+1 \) has two distinct roots between 0 and 1.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $a,b,c,d\in\mathbb Z$ with $a>0$ and
\[
\gcd(a,b,c,d)=1 .
\]
Determine the least positive integer $a$ for which one can find
integers $b,c,d$ and three pairwise-distinct rational numbers
\[
2<r<s<t<3
\]
such that the cubic polynomial
\[
P(x)=a\,x^{3}-b\,x^{2}+c\,x-d
\]
satisfies
\[
P(r)=P(s)=P(t)=0 .
\]
Give that minimal value of $a$, exhibit an explicit polynomial that
attains it, and prove rigorously that no smaller positive value of $a$
is possible.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
1.  Notation and Vieta relations  

Write all three roots in lowest terms,
\[
r=\frac{p_{1}}{q_{1}},\qquad
s=\frac{p_{2}}{q_{2}},\qquad
t=\frac{p_{3}}{q_{3}},\qquad
\gcd(p_{i},q_{i})=1,\;q_{i}>0 .
\tag{1}
\]
Put
\[
\Sigma_{1}=r+s+t,\qquad
\Sigma_{2}=rs+rt+st,\qquad
\Sigma_{3}=rst .
\]
Because
\[
P(x)=a(x-r)(x-s)(x-t),
\]
Vieta's formulas yield
\begin{equation}
b=a\Sigma_{1},\qquad c=a\Sigma_{2},\qquad d=a\Sigma_{3}.
\tag{2}
\end{equation}

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Two crucial arithmetic lemmas  

Lemma 1 (Uniqueness of denominator $2$).
In the open interval $(2,3)$ there is exactly one reduced fraction with
denominator $2$, namely $5/2$.

Proof.  A number $p/2\in(2,3)$ satisfies $4<p<6$.
For odd $p$ this forces $p=5$. \blacksquare   

Lemma 2 (Complete denominator divisibility).
With the notation of (1) one has
\[
q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}\mid a .
\tag{3}
\]

Proof.  Clearing denominators inside
\[
P(x)=a(x-r)(x-s)(x-t)
     =\frac{a}{q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}}\,
       \bigl(q_{1}x-p_{1}\bigr)
       \bigl(q_{2}x-p_{2}\bigr)
       \bigl(q_{3}x-p_{3}\bigr),
\]
we see that every coefficient of
$\dfrac{a}{q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}}
       (q_{1}x-p_{1})(q_{2}x-p_{2})(q_{3}x-p_{3})$
must be an integer.  Since the three linear factors are pairwise
coprime in $\mathbb Z[x]$, the content of their product is $1$, hence
$q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}\mid a$. \blacksquare   

Corollary.
Every denominator satisfies $q_{i}\mid a$ and $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}\le a$.
Because each $q_{i}\ge 2$, necessarily
\[
a\ge q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}\ge 8 .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Searching for small values of $a$  

We prove that $a<18$ is impossible.  
By Lemma 2 we must have $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}\mid a$ and
$q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}\le a\le 17$.  Thus $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}$ can only be one
of the positive integers less than $18$ that can be expressed as a
product of three integers each $\ge 2$.  A short factor check gives the
complete list
\[
\boxed{8,\;12,\;16}.
\tag{4}
\]

We rule out each possibility.

(i)  $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}=8$.  
The only way to write $8$ as a product of three integers
$q_{i}\ge 2$ is $2\cdot2\cdot2$, forcing $q_{1}=q_{2}=q_{3}=2$.
Lemma 1 then allows at most one root with denominator $2$, contradicting the
requirement that $r,s,t$ be distinct.

(ii) $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}=12$.  
Here $12=2\cdot2\cdot3$, so two denominators equal $2$.
Again Lemma 1 violates distinctness.

(iii) $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}=16$.  
The only factorisation with all factors at least $2$ is
$2\cdot2\cdot4$, giving two denominators $2$, contradiction as before.

Therefore no triple $(q_{1},q_{2},q_{3})$ is compatible with
$a\le 17$.  Consequently
\[
a\ge 18 .
\tag{5}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Sharpness: an explicit example with $a=18$  

Choose
\[
r=\frac{5}{2},\qquad
s=\frac{7}{3},\qquad
t=\frac{8}{3},
\]
all lying in $(2,3)$ and pairwise distinct.  Here
$(q_{1},q_{2},q_{3})=(2,3,3)$ and $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}=18$.

Compute the symmetric sums:
\[
\Sigma_{1}=r+s+t=\frac{15}{2},\qquad
\Sigma_{2}=rs+rt+st=\frac{337}{18},\qquad
\Sigma_{3}=rst=\frac{140}{9}.
\]
Insert $a=18$ in (2):
\[
(b,c,d)=\bigl(18\Sigma_{1},\;18\Sigma_{2},\;18\Sigma_{3}\bigr)
      =(135,\,337,\,280).
\]
Thus
\[
P(x)=18x^{3}-135x^{2}+337x-280
     =(2x-5)(3x-7)(3x-8).
\]
A direct check gives
$\gcd(18,135,337,280)=1$, so every problem requirement is fulfilled
with $a=18$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Minimality  

Section 3 proves no polynomial with the required properties exists for
$a\le 17$, while Section 4 supplies one for $a=18$.  Hence
\[
\boxed{a_{\min}=18}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Final answer  

A minimal polynomial is
\[
P(x)=18x^{3}-135x^{2}+337x-280
     =(2x-5)(3x-7)(3x-8),
\]
whose distinct rational zeros $r=5/2$, $s=7/3$, $t=8/3$ all lie in
$(2,3)$.  No smaller positive leading coefficient is possible, so
$a_{\min}=18$. \blacksquare 

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original quadratic task, the enhanced problem is
substantially more intricate for several reasons.

• Higher degree & more variables  
 A cubic replaces a quadratic, so one must manage three roots and an
 additional coefficient, greatly enlarging the algebraic complexity.

• Rational-root requirement  
 The explicit stipulation that the zeros be rational forces a delicate
 number-theoretic analysis of denominators, invoking least common
 multiples, divisibility constraints on a, and a careful case distinction
 for which denominators can even occur in (2,3).

• Multiple simultaneous constraints  
 Besides placing the zeros in (2,3) the problem now demands (i) primitive
 coefficients, (ii) positivity of P at both endpoints of the interval,
 and (iii) simple (non-repeated) zeros, which together rule out many
 otherwise plausible constructions.

• Deeper theoretical ingredients  
 The solution requires Vieta’s relations, discriminant considerations,
 detailed rational-interval counting, and elementary but non-trivial
 divisibility arguments to prove the lower bound and to certify that 18
 indeed suffices.

• Non-routine construction  
 An explicit polynomial had to be engineered so that several numerical
 conditions (integrality, gcd, sign conditions) all hold at once; this
 necessitates non-obvious simultaneous control of three symmetric sums.

Taken together, these added layers of algebra, number theory and careful
construction make the enhanced variant markedly harder than both the
original and the earlier kernel version.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $a,b,c,d\in\mathbb Z$ with $a>0$ and
\[
\gcd(a,b,c,d)=1 .
\]
Determine the least positive integer $a$ for which one can find
integers $b,c,d$ and three pairwise-distinct rational numbers
\[
2<r<s<t<3
\]
such that the cubic polynomial
\[
P(x)=a\,x^{3}-b\,x^{2}+c\,x-d
\]
satisfies
\[
P(r)=P(s)=P(t)=0 .
\]
Give that minimal value of $a$, exhibit an explicit polynomial that
attains it, and prove rigorously that no smaller positive value of $a$
is possible.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
1.  Notation and Vieta relations  

Write all three roots in lowest terms,
\[
r=\frac{p_{1}}{q_{1}},\qquad
s=\frac{p_{2}}{q_{2}},\qquad
t=\frac{p_{3}}{q_{3}},\qquad
\gcd(p_{i},q_{i})=1,\;q_{i}>0 .
\tag{1}
\]
Put
\[
\Sigma_{1}=r+s+t,\qquad
\Sigma_{2}=rs+rt+st,\qquad
\Sigma_{3}=rst .
\]
Because
\[
P(x)=a(x-r)(x-s)(x-t),
\]
Vieta's formulas yield
\begin{equation}
b=a\Sigma_{1},\qquad c=a\Sigma_{2},\qquad d=a\Sigma_{3}.
\tag{2}
\end{equation}

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Two crucial arithmetic lemmas  

Lemma 1 (Uniqueness of denominator $2$).
In the open interval $(2,3)$ there is exactly one reduced fraction with
denominator $2$, namely $5/2$.

Proof.  A number $p/2\in(2,3)$ satisfies $4<p<6$.
For odd $p$ this forces $p=5$. \blacksquare   

Lemma 2 (Complete denominator divisibility).
With the notation of (1) one has
\[
q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}\mid a .
\tag{3}
\]

Proof.  Clearing denominators inside
\[
P(x)=a(x-r)(x-s)(x-t)
     =\frac{a}{q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}}\,
       \bigl(q_{1}x-p_{1}\bigr)
       \bigl(q_{2}x-p_{2}\bigr)
       \bigl(q_{3}x-p_{3}\bigr),
\]
we see that every coefficient of
$\dfrac{a}{q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}}
       (q_{1}x-p_{1})(q_{2}x-p_{2})(q_{3}x-p_{3})$
must be an integer.  Since the three linear factors are pairwise
coprime in $\mathbb Z[x]$, the content of their product is $1$, hence
$q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}\mid a$. \blacksquare   

Corollary.
Every denominator satisfies $q_{i}\mid a$ and $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}\le a$.
Because each $q_{i}\ge 2$, necessarily
\[
a\ge q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}\ge 8 .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Searching for small values of $a$  

We prove that $a<18$ is impossible.  
By Lemma 2 we must have $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}\mid a$ and
$q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}\le a\le 17$.  Thus $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}$ can only be one
of the positive integers less than $18$ that can be expressed as a
product of three integers each $\ge 2$.  A short factor check gives the
complete list
\[
\boxed{8,\;12,\;16}.
\tag{4}
\]

We rule out each possibility.

(i)  $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}=8$.  
The only way to write $8$ as a product of three integers
$q_{i}\ge 2$ is $2\cdot2\cdot2$, forcing $q_{1}=q_{2}=q_{3}=2$.
Lemma 1 then allows at most one root with denominator $2$, contradicting the
requirement that $r,s,t$ be distinct.

(ii) $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}=12$.  
Here $12=2\cdot2\cdot3$, so two denominators equal $2$.
Again Lemma 1 violates distinctness.

(iii) $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}=16$.  
The only factorisation with all factors at least $2$ is
$2\cdot2\cdot4$, giving two denominators $2$, contradiction as before.

Therefore no triple $(q_{1},q_{2},q_{3})$ is compatible with
$a\le 17$.  Consequently
\[
a\ge 18 .
\tag{5}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Sharpness: an explicit example with $a=18$  

Choose
\[
r=\frac{5}{2},\qquad
s=\frac{7}{3},\qquad
t=\frac{8}{3},
\]
all lying in $(2,3)$ and pairwise distinct.  Here
$(q_{1},q_{2},q_{3})=(2,3,3)$ and $q_{1}q_{2}q_{3}=18$.

Compute the symmetric sums:
\[
\Sigma_{1}=r+s+t=\frac{15}{2},\qquad
\Sigma_{2}=rs+rt+st=\frac{337}{18},\qquad
\Sigma_{3}=rst=\frac{140}{9}.
\]
Insert $a=18$ in (2):
\[
(b,c,d)=\bigl(18\Sigma_{1},\;18\Sigma_{2},\;18\Sigma_{3}\bigr)
      =(135,\,337,\,280).
\]
Thus
\[
P(x)=18x^{3}-135x^{2}+337x-280
     =(2x-5)(3x-7)(3x-8).
\]
A direct check gives
$\gcd(18,135,337,280)=1$, so every problem requirement is fulfilled
with $a=18$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Minimality  

Section 3 proves no polynomial with the required properties exists for
$a\le 17$, while Section 4 supplies one for $a=18$.  Hence
\[
\boxed{a_{\min}=18}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Final answer  

A minimal polynomial is
\[
P(x)=18x^{3}-135x^{2}+337x-280
     =(2x-5)(3x-7)(3x-8),
\]
whose distinct rational zeros $r=5/2$, $s=7/3$, $t=8/3$ all lie in
$(2,3)$.  No smaller positive leading coefficient is possible, so
$a_{\min}=18$. \blacksquare 

--------------------------------------------------------------------
